pi_items.source: Cross-checked against task description ('Save as circulatory_system_lesson.flipchart'), setup_task.ps1 ($targetFile variable), and export_result.ps1 ($expectedFile variable). All three match exactly.

Cross-checked against task description ('in C:\Users\Docker\Documents\Flipcharts\'), setup_task.ps1 (New-Item creates this directory), and export_result.ps1 (Get-ChildItem checks this directory). Live-tested: directory exists and is writable in the VM (Test-Path returned True).

Concatenation of expected_directory + expected_filename. Verified present in setup_task.ps1 and export_result.ps1. Live-tested: export_result.ps1 output JSON showed expected_path matching this value exactly.

Task description explicitly specifies 4 pages: Page 1 (Title), Page 2 (Dual-Loop Flow Diagram), Page 3 (Blood Vessel Cross-Sections), Page 4 (Comparison Table). Counted 4 'Page' references in description.

Programmatically verified: all 14 terms appear in the task description string. Each is a required label or heading that the agent must type into the flipchart. Anatomical terms verified against StatPearls and standard cardiovascular physiology references.

Manual count from task description: Page 2 requires 3 rectangles + 4 arrows + 2 circles = 9 shapes. Page 3 requires 5 circles (2 concentric pairs + 1 capillary). Page 4 requires minimum 10 lines/rectangles for a 4x5 table grid. Total non-text shapes: ~24. With text objects counted as shapes (standard in ActivInspire XML), total objects exceed 70. The claim of min_shapes=30 is conservative and verified as a reasonable lower bound.

Task description requires rectangles (HEART, LUNGS, BODY TISSUES boxes; table cells), circles (Gas Exchange markers; vessel cross-sections), and lines/arrows (connecting arrows between organs; table grid lines). All three shape types confirmed available in ActivInspire toolbar via live visual grounding test.

Mixed venous oxygen saturation (SvO2) measured in the pulmonary artery is normally 70-75% (StatPearls NBK564395; AccessAnesthesiology, McGraw Hill). The value of 75% represents the upper end of the normal range for mixed venous blood. Verified via NCBI Bookshelf: 'Normal mixed venous oxygen saturation is about 70-75%'.

Normal pulmonary artery systolic pressure is 18-25 mmHg (American Heart Association; AAFP). The claimed value of 25 mmHg represents the systolic pressure, which is at the upper limit of normal. Mean PAP is 12-16 mmHg. Using systolic (25) is standard for educational contexts. Verified via AHA: 'normal pulmonary artery systolic pressure at rest is 18 to 25 mm Hg'.
The pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs (StatPearls NBK525948; Wikipedia Pulmonary_circulation). Direction: Heart to Lungs is anatomically correct.

Pulmonary vein oxygen saturation is approximately 95-100%. The pulmonary artery wedge position (reflecting pulmonary vein blood) has a mean oxygen saturation of 98.2% with range 90-100% (Brainly/medical references). The value of 100% is at the upper end of normal and commonly used in educational diagrams to represent fully oxygenated blood. Verified via StatPearls and ScienceDirect: pulmonary venous blood is fully oxygenated at 95-100%.

Normal pulmonary venous pressure is approximately 5-8 mmHg (cvphysiology.com; StatPearls NBK557748). Normal PCWP (pulmonary capillary wedge pressure, equivalent to pulmonary vein/left atrial pressure) is 4-12 mmHg. The value of 8 mmHg falls within the normal range. Verified via cvphysiology.com: 'pulmonary venous pressure is about 8 mmHg'.
Pulmonary veins transport oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium of the heart (StatPearls NBK525948; Wikipedia). Direction: Lungs to Heart is anatomically correct.

Normal arterial oxygen saturation (SaO2) is 95-100%. The aorta carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle. Mean radial arterial blood oxygen saturation is 97.3% with range 95-99% (StatPearls). The value of 98% is within normal physiological range. Verified via multiple medical references.

Normal systolic blood pressure is 120 mmHg (AHA blood pressure classification). This is the universally recognized normal systolic pressure value. The aorta receives blood at systolic pressure from the left ventricle. Verified via American Heart Association guidelines.

The aorta carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle to the rest of the body (systemic circulation). Verified via StatPearls and standard anatomy references. Direction: Heart to Body is anatomically correct.

The vena cava carries deoxygenated blood from the body back to the right atrium. Central venous oxygen saturation (ScvO2) measured in the superior vena cava is normally 70-80%, closely approximating mixed venous saturation of 65-75% (StatPearls NBK564395; LITFL). The value of 75% is within normal range. Verified via NCBI/StatPearls.

Normal central venous pressure (CVP, measured in the venae cavae near the right atrium) is 2-8 mmHg (StatPearls NBK519493; Wikipedia Central_venous_pressure). The value of 5 mmHg is within the normal range and represents a typical resting CVP. Verified via StatPearls: 'normal CVP readings typically fall within the range of 2 to 8 mmHg'.
The venae cavae (superior and inferior) return deoxygenated blood from the body to the right atrium. Verified via StatPearls and standard anatomy. Direction: Body to Heart is anatomically correct.

privileged_info_summary: This task requires creating a 4-page ActivInspire flipchart saved as 'circulatory_system_lesson.flipchart' in C:\Users\Docker\Documents\Flipcharts\. Page 1 is a title page with heading 'The Circulatory System: Pulmonary & Systemic Loops' and subtitle 'Blood Flow and Oxygen Transport'. Page 2 contains a dual-loop circulatory diagram with three rectangles arranged vertically: LUNGS (top), HEART (center), BODY TISSUES (bottom), connected by 4 color-coded arrows — blue arrows for deoxygenated blood (Pulmonary Artery going up from Heart to Lungs, Vena Cava going up from Body Tissues to Heart) and red arrows for oxygenated blood (Pulmonary Vein going down from Lungs to Heart, Aorta going down from Heart to Body Tissues). Two green circles labeled 'Gas Exchange' should appear near Lungs and Body Tissues. Labels 'Pulmonary Loop' and 'Systemic Loop' identify each circuit. Page 3 shows three blood vessel cross-sections as concentric circles: Artery has a thick red outer ring with small white center (thick muscular wall, small lumen), Vein has a thin blue outer ring with larger white center (thin wall, large lumen), Capillary is a noticeably smaller circle with thin outline (one cell thick). Each is labeled with vessel name and key characteristic. Page 4 has a data table titled 'Blood Vessel Oxygen & Pressure Data' with 4 columns (Vessel, O2%, Pressure mmHg, Direction) and 4 data rows: Pulmonary Artery (75%, 25 mmHg, Heart to Lungs), Pulmonary Vein (100%, 8 mmHg, Lungs to Heart), Aorta (98%, 120 mmHg, Heart to Body), Vena Cava (75%, 5 mmHg, Body to Heart). All physiological values have been independently verified against medical references (StatPearls, AHA, Guyton & Hall) and fall within accepted normal ranges. The flipchart must contain at least 30 shape objects across all pages.
